questions.question_text: Two unmarried friends buy a Delaware property together without specifying the form of ownership. How does the law classify their estate?
questions.explanation: In Delaware, when co-owners do not specify the form of ownership, the default is tenancy in common. TIC allows unequal shares and does not include a right of survivorship. Each owner can transfer their interest independently.

questions.question_text: A married couple holds Delaware property as tenants by the entirety. A creditor obtains a judgment against only the wife. Can the creditor force a sale of the property?
questions.correct_answer: No, TBE protects against one spouse's creditors
distractors: Yes, the creditor can force a full sale
Yes, but only the wife's half can be sold
Only if the judgment exceeds the home value
questions.explanation: Tenancy by the entirety provides creditor protection for married couples in Delaware. A creditor of only one spouse cannot force the sale or attach the property. Both spouses must be jointly liable for a creditor to reach TBE property.

questions.question_text: Which four unities must be present to create a joint tenancy with right of survivorship in Delaware?
questions.explanation: JTWROS requires the four unities of time (acquired simultaneously), title (same deed), interest (equal shares), and possession (equal right to use). If any unity is broken, the joint tenancy may convert to a tenancy in common.

questions.question_text: A joint tenant in a Delaware JTWROS dies. What happens to the deceased's interest in the property?
questions.correct_answer: It passes to the surviving joint tenant
distractors: It goes to the deceased's heirs by will
It reverts to the state of Delaware
It is divided equally among all heirs
questions.explanation: The right of survivorship is the defining feature of JTWROS. When one joint tenant dies, their interest automatically passes to the surviving joint tenant(s) outside of probate. The deceased's will has no effect on JTWROS property.

questions.question_text: An unmarried couple wants survivorship rights but equal creditor exposure. Which ownership form fits?
questions.explanation: TBE is only available to married couples, so it is not an option here. JTWROS provides survivorship rights for any co-owners. Unlike TBE, it does not shield against one owner's individual creditors.

questions.question_text: Which deed provides the greatest protection to a Delaware buyer?
questions.explanation: A general warranty deed provides full covenants protecting the buyer against all title defects, including those arising before the grantor's ownership. It offers the broadest protection compared to special warranty or quitclaim deeds.

questions.question_text: A seller conveys property using a special warranty deed. A title defect from 20 years ago surfaces. Is the seller liable?
questions.correct_answer: No, only defects during seller's ownership apply
distractors: Yes, the seller guarantees full title history
Yes, but only for recorded defects
No, because all deeds limit liability equally
questions.explanation: A special warranty deed only warrants against defects arising during the grantor's period of ownership. Defects from before the seller acquired the property are not covered. The buyer would need to rely on title insurance for older claims.

questions.question_text: A properly signed Delaware deed is delivered but never recorded. A subsequent buyer records first without knowledge of the earlier sale. Who prevails?
questions.correct_answer: The subsequent buyer who recorded first
distractors: The first buyer with the earlier deed
Neither buyer until a court decides
questions.explanation: Delaware follows a race-notice recording system. A subsequent purchaser who records first and had no notice of the prior unrecorded deed will prevail. This system rewards prompt recording and protects buyers who act in good faith.

questions.question_text: What must a Delaware deed include to be eligible for recording?
distractors: Only the grantor's signature is needed
Signatures of both buyer and seller
questions.explanation: For a deed to be recorded in Delaware, it must be signed by the grantor and acknowledged (notarized). Recording provides constructive notice to the public and establishes priority under Delaware's race-notice system.

questions.question_text: A quitclaim deed is used to transfer property between family members. What warranties does the grantee receive?
questions.explanation: A quitclaim deed conveys whatever interest the grantor may have, if any, with no warranties at all. It is commonly used for transfers between family members, divorcing spouses, or to clear cloud on title where trust exists between the parties.

questions.question_text: In Delaware, who typically conducts real estate closings?
questions.explanation: Delaware is an attorney state, meaning attorneys conduct real estate closings. The closing attorney prepares and reviews documents, ensures proper title transfer, handles escrow funds, and oversees deed recording.

questions.question_text: A buyer's lender requires title insurance. Which policies are standard in a Delaware residential transaction?
questions.correct_answer: Both an owner's and a lender's policy
distractors: Only a lender's policy is standard
Only an owner's policy is standard
Title insurance is optional in Delaware
questions.explanation: In Delaware residential transactions, both an owner's title insurance policy and a lender's policy are standard practice. The owner's policy protects the buyer's equity, while the lender's policy protects the mortgage holder's interest.

questions.question_text: A buyer is purchasing a vacant lot in rural Delaware with no public sewer access. What disclosure is required?
questions.correct_answer: Notice Regarding Sewer and Water service
questions.explanation: For unimproved land in Delaware, a Notice Regarding Sewer/Water is required to inform the buyer that a septic system and/or private well may be needed. This ensures buyers understand utility limitations before committing to the purchase.

questions.question_text: A buyer's agent discovers the rural property has no public water or sewer lines. What should the agent advise?
questions.correct_answer: A private well and septic system may be needed
distractors: The seller must install public utilities
The county will extend sewer lines for free
Financing will be unavailable for the lot
questions.explanation: When purchasing unimproved land without public sewer or water, the buyer should expect to install a private well and septic system. Delaware requires the Notice Regarding Sewer/Water so buyers are fully informed of these potential costs.

questions.question_text: Why does Delaware require attorneys to conduct real estate closings rather than allowing title companies alone?
questions.correct_answer: To ensure legal review of documents and title
distractors: To reduce the overall cost of transactions
To speed up the closing timeline
To eliminate the need for title insurance
questions.explanation: As an attorney state, Delaware mandates attorney involvement to protect both parties through professional legal review of closing documents, title examination, and proper conveyance. This requirement does not eliminate the need for title insurance.
